summary: McKenzie recounts returning to his office to find his development machine destroyed by a power surge, the third computer lost in a week to the same cause. Despite losing $10,000 in hardware, management kept plugging machines into the faulty outlet without investigating. McKenzie's suggestion to check the electrical outlet with a voltmeter confirmed dangerous power spikes. The incident left his entire team idled, giving him unexpected free time to blog.
key_points: Three computers were destroyed in one week by power surges from a faulty electrical outlet, with no one investigating the root cause between failures
Management's failure to diagnose the problem before plugging in additional machines wasted $10,000 in hardware
A simple voltmeter check — prompted by McKenzie's suggestion — immediately identified the issue
The incident highlights the gap between engineering thinking (isolate the cause) and management thinking (keep plugging things in)
money_quotes: After losing $10,000 of hardware I personally would have, hmm, tried to isolate the cause of the problem before frying additional machines but, hey, thats why I'm an engineer and not management.
The reason we have no computers plugged in on this table is that they are all yakiniku.
The odds of me meeting my day job's next deadline are now about 10,000:1 against (100% of my team is now idled) and I get a day or two to catch up on blogging.
Hey, boss, I took an electrical engineering course in college. Maybe the socket here is getting power spikes.
Ha, ha, good thing nothing was plugged in here, otherwise your computers would be yakiniku.
key_insight: When repeated failures share the same symptoms, the engineering instinct to diagnose root cause before continuing operations is worth more than the management instinct to just keep going.
